news_summary: Innovative scientific advancements are transforming waste into valuable resources and harnessing natural systems to combat climate change and promote sustainability.
news_summary_fr: Des avancées scientifiques innovantes transforment les déchets en ressources précieuses et exploitent les systèmes naturels pour lutter contre le changement climatique et promouvoir le développement durable.
news_summary_es: Los avances científicos innovadores están transformando los residuos en recursos valiosos y aprovechando los sistemas naturales para combatir el cambio climático y promover la sostenibilidad.

articles.title: Scientists turn tofu and cheese waste into tiny CO2-catching beads
articles.summary: Scientists have developed biodegradable protein beads made from dairy and tofu waste that can capture carbon dioxide from the atmosphere more efficiently than many current technologies. Unlike conventional systems that require large amounts of energy, the new method releases captured CO2 using a simple room-temperature process.
articles.content:
Limiting long-term global warming to below 1.5°C will require far more than cutting greenhouse gas emissions. According to climate scenarios outlined in the latest Assessment Report from the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC), the world will also need technologies capable of removing and storing hundreds of billions of tons of carbon dioxide (CO 2 ) already in the atmosphere.

One approach receiving growing attention is direct air capture (DAC), a process that removes CO 2 directly from the air. Companies and research teams have spent years developing DAC systems, and ETH Zurich spin-off Climeworks, founded in 2009, was among the first to bring the technology to market. Despite this progress, capturing carbon from the atmosphere remains expensive and requires large amounts of energy.

Protein Beads Made From Food Industry Waste

Researchers at ETH Zurich have now developed a new carbon capture material made from an unexpected source: waste products from dairy and tofu manufacturing.

In a study published in PNAS, a team led by materials scientist Raffaele Mezzenga, a professor in ETH Zurich's Department of Health Sciences and Technology, describes a method that uses whey and byproducts from tofu production to absorb CO 2 .

Large amounts of protein-rich liquid are generated during dairy and tofu production. Only a portion is reused in food manufacturing, while much of the remainder is discarded. The researchers extracted proteins from this waste stream and assembled them into long thread-like structures known as amyloid fibrils.

These fibrils were then combined with potassium hydroxide and formed into porous beads measuring about half a centimeter to one centimeter in diameter.

"The resulting material is like a sponge that can absorb large quantities of CO 2 via the potassium hydroxide," Mezzenga explains.

Carbon Capture Performance Exceeds Existing Methods

When exposed to air, the potassium hydroxide inside the beads reacts with CO 2 , producing hydrogen carbonate, a salt of carbonic acid. This reaction effectively removes carbon dioxide from the atmosphere.

"In our tests with ambient air, we were able to extract 97 milligrams of CO 2 with one gram of material," explains Zhou Dong, a postdoctoral researcher in Mezzenga's group and lead author of the study.

According to Dong, that performance is exceptionally strong, exceeding the capacity of conventional DAC technologies by 10 to 50 percent. He estimates that one kilogram of the protein beads could theoretically capture and isolate about 100 grams of CO 2 during a single operating cycle.

Lower Energy Carbon Removal

Traditional direct air capture systems typically rely on heat and negative pressure to release captured CO 2 from the materials that hold it. The recovered carbon dioxide can then be stored or converted into other products, keeping it out of the atmosphere over the long term.

Because this process consumes significant amounts of energy, DAC facilities are generally most practical in locations with abundant renewable energy resources.

The ETH Zurich team developed a different approach. To release the captured CO 2 , the researchers alternately spray the protein beads with a mild acid and a mild base for roughly 10 minutes at room temperature. This process breaks the chemical bonds holding the CO 2 , allowing it to be collected.

Reusable Beads Support a Circular Economy

The acid, base, and protein beads can all be reused.

"The synthetic materials that are used to capture CO 2 today decompose quickly," says Dong. "By contrast, our protein beads remain stable for a long time."

Laboratory tests showed that the material maintained its performance through 30 cycles of carbon capture and release, with no major loss of efficiency.

Over time, the adsorption capacity would eventually decline. Mezzenga estimates that replacement might be necessary after several thousand cycles. However, because the beads are entirely organic, they could then be repurposed as agricultural fertilizer or converted into biofuel.

Their biodegradable nature could allow the technology to fit into a broader circular economy model, reducing waste while continuing to provide value after the beads are retired from carbon capture use.

"The materials we use for this process are non-toxic and are food-grade," Mezzenga points out.

The team also conducted a life cycle analysis and found that the new approach creates less environmental pollution over its full lifespan than existing DAC technologies.

Can the Technology Scale Up?

Although the results are promising, additional testing will be needed to determine whether the technology can operate effectively on an industrial scale while maintaining its high carbon capture capacity.

For the current study, researchers worked in a controlled laboratory setting using only a few grams of material and captured roughly 50 grams of CO 2 .

Mezzenga remains optimistic about the technology's future. He has spent nearly two decades studying amyloid fibrils and has previously used them to develop biodegradable plastic alternatives and water purification technologies.

"We're confident that the technology is scalable," he says.

According to Mezzenga, the spray-based system used to release CO 2 is compatible with industrial techniques that are already widely used. Dong will continue investigating how the process performs at larger scales.

The researchers have not yet calculated the exact cost of capturing a ton of CO 2 using the new material. Even so, Mezzenga expects it to be substantially less expensive than conventional direct air capture systems.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough in carbon capture technology using biodegradable beads made from dairy and tofu waste. This innovation offers a more energy-efficient, cost-effective, and environmentally friendly method to remove CO2 from the atmosphere, addressing a critical global challenge of climate change. The technology has broad potential impact for climate action and sustainability, with detailed context on its development, performance, and future scalability.
articles.personality_title: Scientists create biodegradable beads from food waste to capture carbon dioxide
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Limiting global warming means not only reducing new greenhouse gases but also removing carbon dioxide (CO2) already in the air. One way to do this is called direct air capture (DAC), which uses materials to pull CO2 from the atmosphere. Current DAC methods use a lot of energy and can be expensive.

**What happened** – Researchers at ETH Zurich found a new way to capture CO2 using tiny beads made from leftover materials from dairy and tofu production, like whey and other protein-rich waste. They turned these proteins into small, porous beads combined with a chemical called potassium hydroxide. These beads act like sponges that soak up CO2 from the air. The team also developed a simple way to release the captured CO2 at room temperature by spraying the beads with mild acids and bases, so the beads can be used again.

**Impact** – These protein beads capture 10 to 50 percent more CO2 than many current methods. They work without needing a lot of heat or energy, which makes the process cheaper and greener. Because the beads are biodegradable and non-toxic, they can be reused many times and eventually turned into fertilizer or biofuel, reducing waste. This approach could help make carbon capture technology more sustainable and affordable.

**What's next step** – The researchers want to test the beads on a larger, industrial scale to see if they work as well outside the lab. They also plan to study the costs and improve the process to make it practical for widespread use. Since the release method uses common industrial spray techniques, scaling up may be possible.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists have developed reusable, biodegradable beads from food waste that capture carbon dioxide efficiently and with less energy, offering a promising new tool to remove CO2 from the air.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques créent des billes biodégradables à partir de déchets alimentaires pour capturer le dioxyde de carbone
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Pour limiter le réchauffement climatique, il faut non seulement réduire les nouvelles émissions de gaz à effet de serre, mais aussi éliminer le dioxyde de carbone (CO2) déjà présent dans l'air. Une méthode pour cela s'appelle la capture directe de l'air (DAC), qui utilise des matériaux pour extraire le CO2. Les méthodes actuelles consomment beaucoup d'énergie et sont coûteuses.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des chercheurs de l'ETH Zurich ont trouvé une nouvelle façon de capturer le CO2 en utilisant de petites billes fabriquées à partir de déchets de production de produits laitiers et de tofu, comme le lactosérum et d'autres protéines. Ils ont transformé ces protéines en petites billes poreuses combinées à de l'hydroxyde de potassium. Ces billes agissent comme des éponges qui absorbent le CO2 de l'air. L'équipe a aussi développé une méthode simple pour libérer le CO2 capturé à température ambiante en pulvérisant les billes avec des acides et des bases doux, ce qui permet de les réutiliser.

**Impact** – Ces billes capturent 10 à 50 % de CO2 en plus que beaucoup de méthodes actuelles. Elles fonctionnent sans besoin de beaucoup de chaleur ou d'énergie, ce qui rend le procédé moins cher et plus écologique. Comme les billes sont biodégradables et non toxiques, elles peuvent être réutilisées plusieurs fois puis transformées en engrais ou en biocarburant, réduisant ainsi les déchets. Cette approche pourrait rendre la technologie de capture du carbone plus durable et abordable.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs souhaitent tester les billes à une échelle industrielle plus grande pour vérifier leur efficacité hors laboratoire. Ils prévoient aussi d'étudier les coûts et d'améliorer le procédé pour le rendre utilisable à grande échelle. La méthode de libération utilisant des techniques de pulvérisation courantes pourrait faciliter cette montée en puissance.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – Des scientifiques ont développé des billes biodégradables et réutilisables à partir de déchets alimentaires qui capturent efficacement le dioxyde de carbone avec moins d'énergie, offrant un nouvel outil prometteur pour éliminer le CO2 de l'air.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos crean perlas biodegradables de desechos alimentarios para capturar dióxido de carbono
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Para limitar el calentamiento global, no basta con reducir los gases de efecto invernadero nuevos, sino que también hay que eliminar el dióxido de carbono (CO2) que ya está en el aire. Una forma de hacerlo es mediante la captura directa del aire (DAC), que utiliza materiales para extraer CO2 de la atmósfera. Los métodos actuales consumen mucha energía y son costosos.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de ETH Zurich encontraron una nueva forma de capturar CO2 usando pequeñas perlas hechas de residuos de la producción de lácteos y tofu, como suero y otras proteínas. Convirtieron estas proteínas en perlas porosas que contienen hidróxido de potasio. Estas perlas actúan como esponjas que absorben CO2 del aire. También desarrollaron un método sencillo para liberar el CO2 capturado a temperatura ambiente rociando las perlas con ácidos y bases suaves, lo que permite reutilizarlas.

**Impacto** – Estas perlas capturan entre un 10 y un 50 % más de CO2 que muchos métodos actuales. Funcionan sin necesitar mucho calor o energía, lo que hace que el proceso sea más barato y ecológico. Como las perlas son biodegradables y no tóxicas, pueden reutilizarse muchas veces y luego convertirse en fertilizante o biocombustible, reduciendo residuos. Este enfoque podría hacer que la captura de carbono sea más sostenible y accesible.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores quieren probar las perlas a escala industrial para ver si funcionan igual de bien fuera del laboratorio. También planean estudiar los costos y mejorar el proceso para hacerlo práctico para un uso generalizado. El método de liberación, que usa técnicas industriales comunes de pulverización, podría facilitar la ampliación.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Científicos han desarrollado perlas biodegradables y reutilizables a partir de residuos alimentarios que capturan dióxido de carbono de manera eficiente y con menos energía, ofreciendo una nueva herramienta prometedora para eliminar CO2 del aire.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of small porous beads made from intertwined protein threads, gently absorbing swirling translucent carbon dioxide particles from the air, set against a soft background of creamy tofu curds and fresh cheese whey flowing like streams, symbolizing sustainable transformation and circular reuse.

articles.title: This unfathomably huge fungal network keeps Earth cool and green
articles.content:
Even if you don’t like eating mushrooms, you’re in debt to fungi. One group of them, known as arbuscular mycorrhizal fungi, form vast subterranean networks of tubes called hyphae, hooking up with the roots of plants to exchange nutrients. Earth is so verdant in large part thanks to these partnerships, as this expansive infrastructure is associated with nearly three-quarters of all plant species. But because the network sprawls underground, it’s been difficult for scientists to determine just how much arbuscular mycorrhizal fungi is out there. (Good luck digging everywhere on the planet and taking samples.)



Scientists have developed a workaround, which has produced some astonishing numbers. Using machine learning models, they’ve estimated that worldwide, the arbuscular mycorrhizal network stretches for 110 quadrillion kilometers, almost a billion times the distance from Earth to the sun. (Scoop up just a teaspoon of soil and you might find 10 meters of fungal strands.) Every year, these fungi shuttle around 4 billion metric tons of carbon, equal to 11 percent of humanity’s CO2 emissions.



Because scientists have already taken thousands upon thousands of samples around the world, the researchers could train the models to build maps (you can play with them here) that predict where these fungi are more or less concentrated, even in the most remote environments. “We have started to have a clear picture of the full extent of these hidden living infrastructures that circulate carbon and nutrients in the soils beneath our feet,” said Toby Kiers, executive director of the Society for the Protection of Underground Networks and coauthor of the new paper, which published today in the journal Science.



In this map, brighter yellow spots indicate higher densities of arbuscular mycorrhizal fungi.

Courtesy of the Society for the Protection of Underground Networks



There are two major classes of mycorrhizal species. The ectomycorrhizal fungi grow as sheaths around a plant’s roots, especially conifer trees, whereas the arbuscular ones in this new paper penetrate them. Either way, these fungi act as an extension of the roots, helping them absorb more water and nutrients. “Just as a circulatory system moves resources through a body, these sort of microscopic fungal pipes are connected to plants,” Kiers said.



In exchange, mycorrhizal species get energy in the form of carbon that the plants have drawn from the atmosphere. They help the plants grow to sequester still more carbon, a mutually beneficial partnership that benefits humans, too, as it keeps the planet from warming even further.



However, the density of arbuscular mycorrhizal fungi isn’t uniform across the planet’s biomes. You might assume that it would be highest in tropical rainforests, but in fact grasslands account for 40 percent of the predicted global arbuscular biomass, the study found. That might be because herbaceous plants like grasses tend to allocate more carbon to their symbiotic fungi than trees do. You can’t see it, but grasslands have vast root systems, meaning there’s loads of hidden biomass. “Even if grasslands get burned above ground, that carbon tends to remain underground, and they can come back again, which is different than forests,” Kiers said.



Yet, Kiers added, just 5 percent of arbuscular mycorrhizal fungal biodiversity hot spots lie in environmentally protected areas. The idea with these new maps is for scientists and policymakers to identify where fungi might be thriving, and protect them. That will simultaneously support plant life and biodiversity overall — all kinds of birds, insects, and herbivores depend on this vegetation, too — and capture still more carbon in the soil. (Some savannas, like Brazil’s cerrado, also store enormous amounts of carbon underground in peat, or dead plant material that resists decay and accumulates over centuries.)

Toby Kiers and Merlin Sheldrake take soil samples in the mountains of Bhutan. Courtesy Tomás Munita

At the other end of the spectrum, the study found that in areas with large-scale agriculture, fungal network densities are about 50 percent lower on average. That may be because synthetic fertilizers provide crops all the nutrients they need, easing their reliance on arbuscular mycorrhizal fungi. Tillage also tears fungal networks apart at the end of a growing season. (Other research has found that tilling also disrupts soil’s ability to retain water.) “Maybe we can do better to have more fungal biomass in our agricultural systems, and in our terrestrial ecosystem as a whole, and capture more carbon dioxide,” said ecologist Smriti Pehim Limbu, who studies mycorrhizal fungi at Dartmouth College but wasn’t involved in the new paper.



Humanity has to feed itself, of course. But with this new data in hand, it can also take steps to protect these critical species hidden underground. “This map is for mycorrhizal fungi what the first detailed maps were for, I don’t know, ocean currents or river systems,” Kiers said. “Where you go from knowing a system exists to knowing where it is, how dense it is, and where it’s threatened.”
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough using machine learning to map the vast global network of arbuscular mycorrhizal fungi, which play a crucial role in carbon sequestration and supporting plant life. This discovery has broad environmental implications for climate action, biodiversity conservation, and sustainable agriculture, providing actionable data for policymakers to protect these vital ecosystems.
articles.personality_title: Scientists map Earth's vast underground fungal network that helps plants and cools the planet
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Fungi called arbuscular mycorrhizal fungi form tiny tubes underground that connect to plant roots. These networks help plants get nutrients and keep the Earth green. But because they are hidden underground, it was hard for scientists to know how big these networks really are.

**What happened** – Scientists used computer programs called machine learning models to estimate the size of these fungal networks worldwide. They found the network stretches about 110 quadrillion kilometers, nearly a billion times the distance from Earth to the sun. These fungi move around 4 billion tons of carbon each year, which is 11% of the carbon dioxide humans produce. The researchers created maps showing where these fungi are most and least common.

**Impact** – This is the first clear picture of how these underground fungi spread across the planet. The fungi help plants grow by giving them water and nutrients, while the plants provide the fungi with energy. This partnership helps store carbon in the soil, which slows down climate change. The study also showed grasslands have more fungal networks than rainforests, and many fungi live outside protected areas, meaning they need more care.

**What's next step** – Scientists and policymakers can now use these maps to protect areas where fungi thrive, especially places not yet protected. They can also work on farming methods that keep fungal networks healthy, which could help capture more carbon and support better soil.

**One-sentence takeaway** – New maps reveal the huge global fungal networks underground that support plants and help reduce climate change by storing carbon in the soil.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les scientifiques cartographient le vaste réseau fongique souterrain qui aide les plantes et refroidit la planète
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Des champignons appelés champignons mycorhiziens arbusculaires forment de minuscules tubes sous terre qui se connectent aux racines des plantes. Ces réseaux aident les plantes à obtenir des nutriments et gardent la Terre verte. Mais comme ils sont cachés sous terre, il était difficile pour les scientifiques de savoir à quel point ces réseaux sont grands.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des scientifiques ont utilisé des programmes informatiques appelés modèles d'apprentissage automatique pour estimer la taille de ces réseaux fongiques dans le monde entier. Ils ont découvert que le réseau s'étend sur environ 110 quadrillions de kilomètres, presque un milliard de fois la distance entre la Terre et le soleil. Ces champignons déplacent environ 4 milliards de tonnes de carbone chaque année, soit 11 % du dioxyde de carbone produit par les humains. Les chercheurs ont créé des cartes montrant où ces champignons sont les plus ou les moins présents.

**Impact** – C'est la première image claire de l'étendue de ces champignons souterrains sur la planète. Les champignons aident les plantes à pousser en leur fournissant de l'eau et des nutriments, tandis que les plantes donnent de l'énergie aux champignons. Ce partenariat aide à stocker le carbone dans le sol, ce qui ralentit le changement climatique. L'étude a aussi montré que les prairies ont plus de réseaux fongiques que les forêts tropicales, et que beaucoup de champignons vivent hors des zones protégées, ce qui signifie qu'ils ont besoin de plus de protection.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques et les décideurs peuvent maintenant utiliser ces cartes pour protéger les zones où les champignons prospèrent, surtout celles qui ne sont pas encore protégées. Ils peuvent aussi travailler sur des méthodes agricoles qui maintiennent ces réseaux fongiques en bonne santé, ce qui pourrait aider à stocker plus de carbone et améliorer les sols.

**Résumé en une phrase** – De nouvelles cartes révèlent les vastes réseaux fongiques souterrains qui soutiennent les plantes et aident à réduire le changement climatique en stockant du carbone dans le sol.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos mapean la enorme red subterránea de hongos que ayuda a las plantas y enfría el planeta
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Hongos llamados hongos micorrízicos arbusculares forman pequeños tubos bajo tierra que se conectan con las raíces de las plantas. Estas redes ayudan a las plantas a obtener nutrientes y mantienen la Tierra verde. Pero como están escondidos bajo tierra, era difícil para los científicos saber qué tan grandes son estas redes.

**Qué pasó** – Los científicos usaron programas de computadora llamados modelos de aprendizaje automático para estimar el tamaño de estas redes de hongos en todo el mundo. Descubrieron que la red se extiende unos 110 cuatrillones de kilómetros, casi mil millones de veces la distancia de la Tierra al sol. Estos hongos transportan alrededor de 4 mil millones de toneladas de carbono cada año, que es el 11 % del dióxido de carbono que producen los humanos. Los investigadores crearon mapas que muestran dónde hay más o menos hongos.

**Impacto** – Esta es la primera imagen clara de cómo estos hongos subterráneos están distribuidos por el planeta. Los hongos ayudan a las plantas a crecer dándoles agua y nutrientes, y las plantas les dan energía. Esta asociación ayuda a almacenar carbono en el suelo, lo que reduce el cambio climático. El estudio también mostró que las praderas tienen más redes de hongos que las selvas tropicales, y muchos hongos viven fuera de áreas protegidas, por lo que necesitan más cuidado.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos y responsables pueden usar estos mapas para proteger las zonas donde los hongos crecen bien, especialmente las que no están protegidas. También pueden mejorar la agricultura para mantener saludables estas redes de hongos, lo que ayudaría a capturar más carbono y mejorar el suelo.

**Resumen en una frase** – Nuevos mapas muestran las enormes redes de hongos subterráneos que apoyan a las plantas y ayudan a reducir el cambio climático almacenando carbono en el suelo.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of an intricate underground network of glowing, thread-like fungal hyphae weaving through rich soil beneath vibrant grassland roots, with soft golden light illuminating the vast, interconnected web symbolizing the hidden fungal infrastructure that sustains plant life and cools the Earth.

articles.title: Scientists built a battery-free device that turns sunlight into fuel
articles.summary: Scientists have developed an artificial photosynthesis system that essentially regulates itself, eliminating the need for batteries used in many current designs. The key innovation is an electrolyzer that automatically adapts to changing sunlight by altering its electrical properties as it heats up. This keeps solar fuel production more stable while reducing cost and complexity.
articles.content:
Researchers at Osaka Metropolitan University have created a new artificial photosynthesis system that can generate solar fuel more consistently while eliminating the need for battery based control equipment. The advance comes from integrating a self regulating chemical component directly into the electrolyzer, reducing both system complexity and cost.

Like natural photosynthesis in plants, artificial photosynthesis uses sunlight to transform water and carbon dioxide into energy rich compounds. One such product is formic acid, a chemical that can serve as a fuel and a way to store energy.

How Artificial Photosynthesis Produces Solar Fuel

At the heart of these systems is an electrolyzer, which converts electricity from solar cells into chemical energy. That energy is then stored in the form of fuels such as formic acid.

A major challenge is maintaining efficient operation when sunlight changes throughout the day. To address this, many artificial photosynthesis systems use Maximum Power Point Tracking (MPPT), a method that continuously adjusts voltage and current so solar cells can deliver the highest possible power output.

However, conventional MPPT setups typically depend on batteries and additional electronic components to smooth out energy flow. While effective, these additions increase both cost and system complexity.

Self Regulating Electrolyzer Eliminates Batteries

To overcome this limitation, a team led by Associate Professor Yasuo Matsubara and Professor Yutaka Amao at the Research Center for Artificial Photosynthesis at Osaka Metropolitan University worked with Iida Group Holdings Co., Ltd to redesign the electrolyzer itself.

Their approach uses a specially designed solid electrolyte built directly into the device. As a result, the electrolyzer can automatically perform the MPPT function on its own, removing the need for battery based control systems.

Rather than relying on external electronics, converters, or batteries, the electrolyzer adjusts its electrical characteristics through its own thermal and impedance properties.

"As sunlight increases, the electrolyzer naturally heats up. The system is designed so that this warming causes the electrical resistance to drop, allowing electricity to flow more freely," Professor Amao explained. "This makes the system automatically adjust its electrical behavior."

"This self-regulating behavior helps keep fuel production more stable throughout the day and automates the system, while reducing dependence on batteries and costly external components," he added.

Stable Formic Acid Production Under Real Sunlight

When the researchers tested the technology under actual outdoor conditions, the system consistently produced formic acid from water and CO2 even as sunlight levels fluctuated.

"We were confident that it would be successful, as we previously showcased this research at the 'Joint Pavilion Iida Group × Osaka Metropolitan University' exhibition as part of the Osaka Kansai Expo 2025," Professor Matsubara said. "It successfully generated enough formic acid to power a miniature diorama in the pavilion, showing its potential as an efficient artificial photosynthesis system that could potentially be used to charge applications in our homes."

The findings were published in EES Solar.
articles.reasoning: The article describes a significant technological breakthrough in artificial photosynthesis that enables stable solar fuel production without batteries, reducing cost and complexity. This innovation has broad implications for renewable energy and sustainable fuel generation, benefiting society by advancing clean energy solutions. The article provides detailed context on the technology, its operation, and real-world testing, fulfilling criteria for positive real-world impact, significance, and substance.
articles.personality_title: New solar fuel device works without batteries by adjusting itself to sunlight
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists are working to create artificial photosynthesis systems that use sunlight to turn water and carbon dioxide into fuels. These systems usually need batteries to control how much power they use, which makes them expensive and complicated.

**What happened** – Researchers at Osaka Metropolitan University developed a new electrolyzer, the part of the system that changes electricity into fuel, which can adjust itself automatically to changing sunlight. Instead of using batteries, it changes its electrical resistance as it heats up from the sun, keeping fuel production steady.

**Impact** – This self-regulating electrolyzer simplifies the system by removing the need for batteries and extra electronic parts. It lowers costs and makes the technology easier to use. Tests showed it could produce formic acid, a useful fuel, consistently even when sunlight changed during the day.

**What’s next step** – The team plans to continue testing and improving this technology. It could be used in homes or other places to create clean fuel directly from sunlight, water, and carbon dioxide, helping develop renewable energy solutions.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists built a solar fuel device that controls itself without batteries by changing its electrical properties as sunlight heats it up, making clean fuel production simpler and more reliable.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un nouvel appareil solaire produit du carburant sans batterie en s’adaptant à la lumière
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques cherchent à créer des systèmes de photosynthèse artificielle qui utilisent la lumière du soleil pour transformer l’eau et le dioxyde de carbone en carburants. Ces systèmes ont souvent besoin de batteries pour contrôler la puissance, ce qui les rend chers et complexes.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de l’Université métropolitaine d’Osaka ont développé un électrolyseur capable de s’ajuster automatiquement à la lumière changeante du soleil. Au lieu d’utiliser des batteries, il modifie sa résistance électrique en chauffant, ce qui permet de maintenir une production stable de carburant.

**Impact** – Cet électrolyseur auto-régulé simplifie le système en supprimant la nécessité de batteries et d’autres composants électroniques. Cela réduit les coûts et facilite l’utilisation de la technologie. Les tests ont montré qu’il pouvait produire de l’acide formique, un carburant utile, de manière constante même lorsque la lumière changeait.

**Prochaine étape** – L’équipe va continuer à tester et améliorer cette technologie. Elle pourrait être utilisée dans les maisons ou ailleurs pour créer du carburant propre directement à partir du soleil, de l’eau et du dioxyde de carbone, aidant au développement des énergies renouvelables.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Des scientifiques ont construit un appareil solaire qui se contrôle lui-même sans batterie en modifiant ses propriétés électriques quand il chauffe au soleil, rendant la production de carburant propre plus simple et fiable.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo dispositivo solar produce combustible sin baterías al adaptarse a la luz
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos trabajan en sistemas de fotosíntesis artificial que usan la luz del sol para convertir agua y dióxido de carbono en combustibles. Estos sistemas suelen necesitar baterías para controlar la energía, lo que los hace caros y complicados.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad Metropolitana de Osaka crearon un electrolizador que puede ajustarse automáticamente a la luz solar cambiante. En vez de usar baterías, cambia su resistencia eléctrica al calentarse con el sol, manteniendo estable la producción de combustible.

**Impacto** – Este electrolizador autorregulado simplifica el sistema al eliminar la necesidad de baterías y componentes electrónicos extras. Esto reduce costos y facilita el uso de la tecnología. Las pruebas mostraron que podía producir ácido fórmico, un combustible útil, de forma constante aunque la luz cambiara.

**Próximo paso** – El equipo continuará probando y mejorando esta tecnología. Podría usarse en hogares u otros lugares para crear combustible limpio directamente del sol, el agua y el dióxido de carbono, ayudando a desarrollar energías renovables.

**Frase clave** – Científicos crearon un dispositivo solar que se controla solo sin baterías al cambiar sus propiedades eléctricas cuando se calienta con el sol, haciendo que la producción de combustible limpio sea más simple y confiable.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a glowing, stylized electrolyzer device shaped like a translucent crystal, seamlessly integrated with a radiant sun above, emitting gentle golden light that flows like liquid energy into the device, which in turn produces shimmering droplets of formic acid symbolized as small luminous orbs rising upward, all set against a softly colored natural background of sky and subtle water ripples, emphasizing the smooth, self-regulating flow of sunlight transforming into fuel without any electronic components.

articles.title: WasteBar turns cigarette butt waste into food currency in the Netherlands
articles.summary:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM There is a small mobile cart somewhere in the Netherlands right now, and if you bring it a handful of cigarette butts, it will give you poffertjes. Those are Dutch mini pancakes, in case you were wondering, and yes, the exchange is real. WasteBar is the project behind it. […]
The post WasteBar turns cigarette butt waste into food currency in the Netherlands first appeared on The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News.
articles.content:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM

There is a small mobile cart somewhere in the Netherlands right now, and if you bring it a handful of cigarette butts, it will give you poffertjes. Those are Dutch mini pancakes, in case you were wondering, and yes, the exchange is real.

WasteBar is the project behind it. You collect litter from the ground around you, bring it to the cart, and get food or drinks in return. Cigarette butts and cans are the main currency, while a portion of poffertjes is one of the rewards.

Why cigarette butts in particular

The Netherlands discards between five and ten billion cigarette butts every year. Each filter is made of cellulose acetate, a plastic that takes up to a decade to break down, leaching nicotine and heavy metals into soil and waterways along the way.

WasteBar partners with artist Angelina Kumar and the organization UPPACT, which recycles plastic waste into new products. Kumar built an installation called Het Peukenbos (The Cigarette Butt Forest) from over 500,000 collected butts, shown in Utrecht through September 2025. The 2026 campaign is going for one million, with the plan to turn them into a recycled bench or garden set through UPPACT.

The education piece

When you show up at the cart, the WasteBar team does not just hand over pancakes. They talk with you about what cigarette butts do to the environment and walk you through how to separate and recycle other kinds of waste. It is low-key about it. You came for the food, and you leave with something extra.

There is a version of this logic that sounds like it should not work. People pick up strangers’ cigarette butts because they get a snack? But the numbers tell a different story. The Netherlands generates an estimated 50 million kilograms (about 110 million pounds) of litter a year, and this cart has figured out how to make the cleanup feel like something.

A bar where the price of entry is the problem itself

The friction in environmental action is usually not valued. Most people are not opposed to a cleaner street. The problem is that cleaning up after other people feels thankless and pointless. Mini pancakes, it turns out, are enough to change that calculation for a lot of people.

You might bring ten cigarette butts. You might bring a hundred. Either way, the street is a bit cleaner, and you got something out of it.

Did this solution stand out? Share it with a friend or support our mission by becoming an Emissary.
articles.source: The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News
articles.reasoning: The article describes a creative and impactful environmental initiative in the Netherlands that incentivizes litter cleanup, specifically cigarette butts, by exchanging them for food. This project addresses a significant pollution problem with plastic waste that harms soil and waterways, involves community engagement and education, and partners with recycling organizations to repurpose collected waste. The initiative has broad environmental benefits and raises public awareness, making it an inspiring and substantive story.
articles.personality_title: In the Netherlands, cigarette butts can be traded for mini pancakes
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The Netherlands produces billions of cigarette butts every year. These butts are made of plastic that takes about ten years to break down and pollutes soil and water.

**What happened** – A project called WasteBar set up a small mobile cart where people can bring cigarette butts and other litter. In return, they receive Dutch mini pancakes called poffertjes or drinks. This exchange encourages people to pick up trash from the streets.

**Impact** – This idea makes cleaning up litter rewarding. Instead of feeling like a thankless chore, people get a tasty snack for helping the environment. WasteBar also teaches visitors about the harm cigarette butts cause and how to recycle properly. The collected butts are recycled into new products like benches.

**What's next step** – WasteBar plans to collect even more cigarette butts and create more recycled items through their partner group UPPACT. The campaign continues to raise awareness and inspire more people to clean up litter.

**One-sentence takeaway** – WasteBar turns harmful cigarette waste into a fun, tasty way to clean streets and protect the environment.
articles.personality_title_fr: Aux Pays-Bas, les mégots de cigarette s'échangent contre des mini crêpes
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les Pays-Bas produisent chaque année des milliards de mégots de cigarette. Ces filtres en plastique mettent environ dix ans à se décomposer et polluent les sols et les eaux.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Un projet nommé WasteBar a installé un petit chariot mobile où les gens peuvent apporter des mégots et des déchets. En échange, ils reçoivent des mini crêpes hollandaises appelées poffertjes ou des boissons. Cet échange encourage les gens à ramasser les déchets dans la rue.

**Impact** – Cette idée rend le nettoyage des déchets gratifiant. Au lieu d'une tâche ingrate, les gens obtiennent une gourmandise pour aider l'environnement. WasteBar informe aussi les visiteurs sur les dangers des mégots et le tri des déchets. Les mégots collectés sont recyclés en nouveaux objets comme des bancs.

**Prochaine étape** – WasteBar prévoit de collecter encore plus de mégots et de fabriquer d'autres objets recyclés avec leur partenaire UPPACT. La campagne continue de sensibiliser et d'inciter plus de personnes à nettoyer les déchets.

**Une phrase clé** – WasteBar transforme les mégots nuisibles en une manière amusante et savoureuse de nettoyer les rues et protéger la nature.
articles.personality_title_es: En los Países Bajos, los colillas de cigarrillo se cambian por mini panqueques
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los Países Bajos generan miles de millones de colillas de cigarrillo cada año. Estos filtros de plástico tardan unos diez años en descomponerse y contaminan el suelo y el agua.

**Qué pasó** – Un proyecto llamado WasteBar instaló un carrito móvil donde las personas pueden llevar colillas y basura. A cambio, reciben mini panqueques holandeses llamados poffertjes o bebidas. Este intercambio anima a la gente a recoger basura de las calles.

**Impacto** – Esta idea hace que limpiar basura sea gratificante. En lugar de ser una tarea sin recompensa, las personas obtienen un bocadillo delicioso por ayudar al medio ambiente. WasteBar también educa a los visitantes sobre el daño de las colillas y cómo reciclar correctamente. Las colillas recogidas se reciclan en nuevos productos como bancos.

**Próximo paso** – WasteBar planea recolectar aún más colillas y fabricar más objetos reciclados con su socio UPPACT. La campaña sigue aumentando la conciencia y motivando a más personas a limpiar la basura.

**Una frase clave** – WasteBar convierte los dañinos desechos de colillas en una forma divertida y sabrosa de limpiar las calles y cuidar el medio ambiente.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a charming mobile cart shaped like a traditional Dutch poffertjes stand, surrounded by a gentle swirl of cigarette butts transforming into golden mini pancakes floating upward, set against a soft, natural-colored backdrop of a clean urban street with subtle silhouettes of people exchanging litter for food, evoking a hopeful, community-driven environmental cleanup.
